title: practical english usage - Part 165
content:
borrow: ►    408.

My hands were blue with cold when I got home blue with cold, red with anger, etc I belong to a local athletics club.

belong to (= be a member of) Those glasses belong on the top shelf.

belong in/on, etc (= go, fit, have its place in/on, etc) I don't believe a word she says.

Don't believe her.

believe a person or something that is said (= accept as truthful/true - If you believe in me, I can do anything.

I half believe in life after death.

believe in God, Santa Claus, etc (= believe that... exists; trust) I'm not bad at tennis.

ask: ►    383.

When did you arrive in England?

What time do we arrive at Cardiff station?

arrive at or in (     to) I must apologise for disturbing you.

I think we should apologise to the Smiths.

apologise to somebody for something She's anxious to find a better job.

anxious + infinitive (= eager, wanting) We're all anxious for an end to this misunderstanding.

anxious for (= eager to have) I'm getting anxious about money.

anxious about (= worried about) What are you so angry about?

angry about (sometimes at) something I'm angry with her for lying to me.

angry with (sometimes at) a person for doing something I'll agree to your suggestion if you lower the price.

agree to a suggestion or demand Let's try to agree on a date.

agree on a matter for decision We agree about/on most things.

agree about/on a subject of discussion I entirely agree with you.

He left the company because he didn't agree with their sales policy.

agree with a person, opinion or policy Are you afraid of spiders?

She accused me of poisoning her dog.

accuse somebody of something (     for) Section18 Prepositions grammar •  213   prepositions after particular words and expressions I've spent the last two weeks fighting with the tax office.

fight, struggle, etc with somebody Could you explain this rule to me?

explain something to somebody (     explain somebody something) Sherry is an example of a fortified wine.

example of (     for) When I entered the room everybody stopped talking.

enter a place (no preposition) We've just entered into an agreement with Carsons Ltd.

enter into an agreement, a discussion, etc That idiot Kim  drove into a tree again yesterday.

drive into (     against) Who's the woman dressed in green?

dress(ed) in (     with) What does it mean if you dream about/of mountains?

dream about/of (while asleep) / often dreamed of being famous when I was younger.

dream of (= think of, imagine) The book is divided into three parts.

divide into (     In) We'd better discuss your travel plans.

Birr [to] discuss something (no preposition) We had a long discussion about politics.

[a] discussion about something You must be pretty disappointed with/at/about your exam results.

disappointed with/at/about something My father never showed if he was disappointed with/in me.

disappointed with/in somebody You won't have much  difficulty (in) getting to know people in Italy.

I'm having difficulty with my travel arrangements.

difficulty with something, (in) doing something (     diffieulties-tθ-...) different: ►    433.

A week after the accident he died from his injuries.

More people died of flu in 1919 than were killed in the First World War.

Write now for details of our special offer.

He doesn 't want to be dependent on his parents.

We may play football - it depends on the weather.

depend/dependent on (     from     of) I wasn't concentrating and 1 crashed into the car in front.

crash into (                 against) He congratulated the team on/for having won all their games.

congratulate/congratulations on/for doing something Congratulations on your new job!

/ must congratulate you on your exam results.

congratulate/congratulations on something grammar •  213   prepositions after particular words and expressions Thanks for looking after me when I was ill.

look after (= take care of) Stop looking at me like that.

look at (= point one's eyes at) If you don’t listen to people, they won’ t listen to you.

/ left London early, before the traffic got too heavy.

leave somewhere (talking about the action of leaving) Does the plane leave from Liverpool or Manchester?
